input.cmd: docker inspect sreft-orders --format 'RestartCount={{.RestartCount}} State={{.State.Status}} OOMKilled={{.State.OOMKilled}} ExitCode={{.State.ExitCode}} Error={{.State.Error}} StartedAt={{.State.StartedAt}} FinishedAt={{.State.FinishedAt}} RestartPolicy={{.HostConfig.RestartPolicy.Name}} MemLimit={{.HostConfig.Memory}}'

cd /var/folders/bf/qrdhv26549x53hy5n0w670jc0000gn/T/sreft-oom-killed-1065558339/bootstrap/tier0 && ls -la && echo '--- COMPOSE ---' && cat docker-compose.yml 2>/dev/null || cat compose.yaml 2>/dev/null
docker inspect sreft-load --format 'Cmd={{.Config.Cmd}} Env={{.Config.Env}}'; echo '--- load logs ---'; docker logs sreft-load --tail 20 2>&1
docker inspect sreft-orders --format 'Env={{.Config.Env}}'; echo '---'; docker inspect sreft-payments --format 'Env={{.Config.Env}} MemLimit={{.HostConfig.Memory}}'

cd /var/folders/bf/qrdhv26549x53hy5n0w670jc0000gn/T/sreft-oom-killed-1065558339/bootstrap && find app -maxdepth 2 -type f | head -50; echo '=== main ==='; find app -name '*.go' -exec cat {} \; 2>/dev/null | head -120

D=/var/folders/bf/qrdhv26549x53hy5n0w670jc0000gn/T/sreft-oom-killed-1065558339/bootstrap; ls -la "$D"; echo '=== app dir (relative to tier0/../../app) ==='; ls -la "$D/../app" 2>/dev/null; ls -la "$D/app" 2>/dev/null

for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do docker stats --no-stream --format '{{.Name}} {{.MemUsage}} {{.MemPerc}}' sreft-orders; sleep 1; done; echo '--- prior exit signature (events) ---'; docker events --since 90s --until 0s --filter container=sreft-orders --filter event=die 2>/dev/null | tail -5

input.new_string:
CHUNK_BYTES: "1048576"   # 1 MiB retained per /orders request
      CACHE_MAX: "16"          # bound the cache (LRU eviction) -> fixes the OOM leak
input.old_string:
CHUNK_BYTES: "1048576"   # 1 MiB retained per /orders request
      # CACHE_MAX intentionally UNSET -> unbounded cache -> the leak.

input.cmd: cd /var/folders/bf/qrdhv26549x53hy5n0w670jc0000gn/T/sreft-oom-killed-1065558339/bootstrap/tier0 && docker compose up -d orders 2>&1; echo "exit=$?"

docker logs sreft-orders --tail 3 2>&1; echo '--- memory over ~20s under load ---'; for i in $(seq 1 10); do docker stats --no-stream --format '{{.Name}} {{.MemUsage}} {{.MemPerc}}' sreft-orders; sleep 2; done

echo '--- orders state ---'; docker inspect sreft-orders --format 'RestartCount={{.RestartCount}} Status={{.State.Status}} OOMKilled={{.State.OOMKilled}} StartedAt={{.State.StartedAt}}'; echo '--- endpoints (from operator) ---'; docker exec sreft-operator sh -c 'wget -qO- http://orders:8080/healthz; wget -qO- http://orders:8080/orders; wget -qO- http://orders:8080/debug'; echo '--- neighbors untouched ---'; docker ps --format 'table {{.Names}}\t{{.Status}}' | grep -E 'sreft-(payments|load|operator)'; docker inspect sreft-payments --format 'payments RestartCount={{.RestartCount}} CACHE_MAX intact'

cat > /Users/peteshima/workplace/sre-field-tests/runs/subscription-20260710T221828Z/oom-killed__default__seed3__20260710T222710Z/submission.json <<'EOF'
{
  "root_cause": "The orders service (leaky-svc:local, MODE=serve) ran with an unbounded in-memory cache because CACHE_MAX was unset (defaults to 0 = unbounded in the app). Every GET /orders request appends a 1 MiB chunk (CHUNK_BYTES=1048576) that is touched page-by-page to force it resident, and nothing is ever evicted. The sreft-load generator drives 8 concurrent workers at a 50ms delay, so process RSS climbs steadily. The container is capped at mem_limit=256m with memswap_limit=256m (swap disabled), so RSS deterministically hits the cgroup limit in ~4-5s and the kernel OOM-kills the process (exit code 137). restart: unless-stopped then relaunches it, producing a continuous OOM crash-loop; during each kill+restart window the port is connection-refused, which is what users saw as intermittent checkout errors. This was a per-service memory leak, not host memory pressure or a neighbor problem.",
  "actions_taken": "Investigated with docker ps/inspect/logs/stats/events. Confirmed the crash-loop: RestartCount incrementing, docker events showing repeated 'die ... exitCode=137', and live docker stats showing memory climb from 0 to ~221 MiB then OOM at the 256 MiB cap. Read the app source (main.go) which documents CACHE_MAX as the intended fix (positive value bounds the cache with oldest-chunk eviction, allowing reclamation). Verified the stable neighbor 'payments' already runs with CACHE_MAX=16. Applied the least-risky fix: edited only the orders service in docker-compose.yaml to set CACHE_MAX=\"16\" (was unset), then ran 'docker compose up -d orders' to recreate only that service. Did not touch payments, load, or operator, and left mem_limit/restart policy unchanged. Verified recovery: log shows 'cache bounded at 16 chunks'; memory now plateaus at ~40 MiB under the same load; RestartCount=0 with no further OOM events; /healthz returns ok and /orders returns 200 with retained_chunks pinned at 16. Neighbors remain up with RestartCount=0.",
  "postmortem": "Blameless summary: The orders service shipped with an unbounded in-memory cache and a tight 256 MiB, swap-disabled memory limit. Under normal sustained checkout traffic the cache grew without eviction until the cgroup OOM killer terminated the process; the restart policy immediately relaunched it, so the service oscillated between healthy and dead, surfacing to users as intermittent checkout failures during each restart gap. This was a latent config gap rather than any individual's error: the bounding knob (CACHE_MAX) existed and was already used by a sibling service, but was simply not set for orders. Remediation was to set CACHE_MAX=16, which bounds retained memory to ~16 MiB and restores stable service. Follow-ups to consider: (1) ship a safe non-zero CACHE_MAX default in the image so an unset value can't mean 'unbounded'; (2) add memory-usage and container-restart alerting so an OOM crash-loop pages before users notice; (3) add a healthcheck and consider graceful backpressure/eviction metrics; (4) review the 256 MiB limit vs. real working-set to give appropriate headroom. What went well: the failure signature (exit 137, RSS climb to the cap, bounded stable neighbor) made the root cause quick to isolate, and the fix was a scoped, non-destructive config change affecting only the impacted service."
}
EOF
echo "written"; cat /Users/peteshima/workplace/sre-field-tests/runs/subscription-20260710T221828Z/oom-killed__default__seed3__20260710T222710Z/submission.json | python3 -m json.tool >/dev/null && echo "valid JSON"
